text: The robotic surgical procedure begins with the placement of ports. The surgeon creates a dissection plane beneath the inferior mesenteric vein. Medial to lateral mobilization of the rectal tube is performed with medial peritoneal cuts. The rectosigmoid and descending colon are reflected off the retroperitoneum. The inferior mesenteric artery is dissected to its root, clipped, and divided beyond the origin of the left colic artery. This is followed by posterior pelvic dissection of the rectal tube.

The surgeon completes lateral peritoneal cuts over the external iliac vessels, facilitating lateral pelvic lymph node dissection. The external iliac vessels are identified and dissected, with the ureter shifted medially. The obliterated umbilical artery is traced to the internal iliac vasculature, creating a paravascular space. Nodal tissue between the obturator internus and internal iliac vasculature is dissected and removed. The medial nodal zone is addressed by creating a space between the internal iliac vasculature and pelvic hypogastric, with nodal tissue carefully dissected off the internal iliac vessel branches and removed.

The surgeon clips and divides the superior vesicle pedicle, followed by the blood vessels supplying the prostate and seminal vesicles. The left ureter is clipped and divided near the bladder, then dissected off the retroperitoneum. The right lateral pelvic node dissection is performed similarly, with the right ureter clipped, divided, and mobilized. The bladder is mobilized, both umbilical arteries are divided, and the bladder is taken down by entering the space of Retzius. The dorsal venous complex is identified, clipped, and divided, with the prostate dissected and lifted off the pelvic floor. Bleeding from the dorsal venous complex is controlled, and the area is packed to maintain a clean surgical field.

The surgeon divides the descending colon using an endostapler and clips the descending colon mesentery to the anterior abdominal wall. The ileocecal junction is mobilized to facilitate the placement of the ileal conduit. Umbilical tape measures the bowel length, and clips mark the proximal and distal ends of the conduit. Endo staplers are used for bowel division, and bowel continuity is established through a side-to-side ileo-ileostomy using endo staplers.

The surgeon spatulates the cut ends of the ureters and joins their medial walls using absorbable suture material. Digest ends are inserted into both ureters with a guide wire, and the other ends are inserted into the distal end of the ileal conduit. Ureteroenteric anastomosis is completed using continuous absorbable sutures. The final part of the excision is completed via the perineal route, and the ileal conduit and end colostomy are fashioned.
